excerpt: Waffles had a great session — really locked in her sit-stay today up to 15 feet.
full_text: Waffles came in with a ton of energy today but channeled it really well once we got into structured work. We focused on duration and distance with her sit-stay. She held it at 15 feet for 8 seconds, which is a big jump from last week. We also worked on "leave it" with high-value treats on the floor — she struggled at first but finished strong. Recommend reinforcing "leave it" at home with kibble before dinner.

excerpt: Good impulse control work today. Waffles is really starting to understand "wait" at doorways.
full_text: Worked on "wait" at thresholds today — doors, gates, and the crate. Waffles needed a few resets at the front door but by the third repetition she was offering the wait behavior without a cue. That's a good sign that it's becoming a default. We also did some leash pressure desensitization — she's been pulling hard on walks and we want to get ahead of that before it becomes a habit. Finished with play as reinforcement, which she loves.

excerpt: Worked on name recognition and auto-check-ins. Waffles is building great focus.
full_text: Today was all about engagement. We ran name recognition drills in a distracting environment — other dogs nearby, toys on the ground — and Waffles did really well. She's starting to offer eye contact spontaneously, which is exactly what we want as a foundation for everything else. Also introduced the "place" cue on a platform. She got on and off a few times confidently.

excerpt: First loose-leash session. Waffles is a strong puller but her attention is there.
full_text: Kicked off Waffles's loose-leash walking program today. She's been pulling pretty hard — classic Golden, wanting to be everywhere at once. We started with a simple "follow me" exercise in the parking lot: I change direction every time she starts to pull, and she gets rewarded every time she checks back in. By the end of the session she was walking beside me for about 10 steps before drifting. That's a solid start for day one.

excerpt: Loose-leash progress is solid. Getting consistent 20-step stretches now.
full_text: Waffles is picking up the loose-leash concept quickly. We got consistent 20-step stretches in a low-distraction environment. Introduced the "let's go" cue to signal a walk is starting. She's still pulling toward other dogs when they pass, but her recovery time is much faster — she's checking back in within 3-4 seconds instead of needing a full stop. Keep practicing in the front yard with a 6-foot leash before coming in.

excerpt: Down-stay duration up to 30 seconds at 10 feet. Waffles is building real patience.
full_text: Focused almost entirely on down-stay duration today. Waffles held it for 30 seconds at 10 feet with moderate distractions — another dog walking past, a toy on the floor. She broke a couple of times early in the session but we calmly reset and she finished strong. The key right now is not pushing distance and duration at the same time. We're keeping distance at 10 feet and only increasing time until she can do 60 seconds reliably.

excerpt: Great recall session in the big yard. Waffles is coming fast and happy.
full_text: Recall day in the big yard — Waffles was a rockstar. We used a long line (20 feet) and practiced calling her away from environmental distractions: a pile of toys, a water bowl, another dog on leash. She came every time. The key thing we reinforced today is that coming when called always means something great happens — a big party, a treat, and then release back to play. Never call to end the fun.

excerpt: Introduction to "place" on a mat. Waffles is building confidence stepping onto novel surfaces.
full_text: New skill today: "place" on a mat. Waffles was slightly uncertain about the mat at first — she's a little sensitive to unfamiliar surfaces — but once she got a few reps of stepping on it and getting rewarded, she started seeking it out. By the end of the session she was running to the mat and lying down. We'll add duration next time.

excerpt: Worked through some overstimulation in group play — Waffles is learning to self-regulate.
full_text: Waffles was pretty amped up today — a new dog in the group got her going. We used that as a training opportunity. When she started to get over threshold (spinning, barking, unable to respond to her name), we calmly removed her for a 2-minute reset in a quiet space, then reintroduced her. Did this three times. By the third introduction she was notably calmer and was able to engage politely. This is exactly the work she needs.

excerpt: Solid session on crate comfort. Waffles is going in voluntarily now.
full_text: Crate training focus today. Waffles had been a bit reluctant to go in on her own — she'd do it on cue but you could tell she wasn't enthusiastic. We spent today making the crate the best place in the room: feeding her meals inside, doing short "in" sessions with high-value treats, and keeping the door open so she could choose to go in. By the end of the session she was choosing to go in on her own between exercises. That's the goal.

excerpt: Lola nailed the 3D stays today — distance, duration, and distraction all at once.
full_text: Lola is performing at a high level. Today we combined distance (20 feet), duration (45 seconds), and distraction (another dog running past) in her down-stay for the first time. She held it all three times. Her release cue is clean and she's not anticipating it anymore. We also worked on "go to place" from 15 feet away — she's getting there.

excerpt: Week 2 of puppy manners. Lola is the most focused dog in the class.
full_text: Week 2 of puppy manners and Lola is already ahead of the curve. She's offering sits without being asked, making eye contact spontaneously, and ignoring food on the floor when asked. The main thing to work on at home is polite greetings — she still jumps on new people. Practice "four on the floor" by having visitors turn away until all four feet are down, then immediately reward.

excerpt: Lola had a great day in day care. Played well with everyone, no incidents.
full_text: Lola was a model citizen in day care today. She played well with the other dogs, took breaks on her own when she needed them, and was easy to recall at pick-up time. She did have one brief moment of resource guarding over a tennis ball — she froze and gave a hard stare when another dog approached. We redirected immediately and she moved on without incident. Worth noting for future play sessions.

excerpt: Off-leash recall in a new environment. Lola came every single time.
full_text: Took Lola's recall work to a new location today — the back field, which has more interesting smells and is less familiar. She came every time, even when there was a squirrel near the fence. That's a big deal. We also introduced a whistle recall as a secondary cue, which she's picking up quickly. Whistle recall is useful because it carries farther and sounds distinct from everything else.

excerpt: Week 1 of puppy manners. Lola was a star — best focus in the group.
full_text: First night of puppy manners class and Lola was immediately the standout. Her foundation is solid — she clearly has good training at home. We covered sit, down, stay intro, name recognition, and polite leash walking. She nailed all of them. The main challenge for Lola in a group class is learning to focus on her owner with other dogs nearby, which is actually harder for trained dogs because they know what they're supposed to do but the environment is novel.

excerpt: Worked on impulse control with food distractions. Lola is rock solid.
full_text: Impulse control session with food distractions: treats on the ground, a filled Kong on a platform, food in an open hand. Lola ignored all of it on a "leave it" cue and waited for the "take it" release. We also did some "zen hand" work — food in the closed fist, wait for the dog to stop trying and back away, then open the hand. She got it in about 4 repetitions. She's a quick learner.

excerpt: Smooth day care day. Lola is a natural pack member.
full_text: Lola had another smooth day in day care. No incidents, good energy management, initiated play politely. She's become a bit of a social anchor for more anxious dogs — she doesn't overwhelm them but she also doesn't back away. Worth noting that she's starting to prefer the more mellow dogs in the group over the high-energy puppies, which makes sense for a 5-year-old.

excerpt: Introduced "settle" on a mat in a busy environment. Lola picked it up fast.
full_text: New skill: "settle" on a mat. This is different from "place" — we're asking for a fully relaxed down, not just stationary. We started in a quiet room, then gradually added distractions. Lola took about 10 minutes to truly relax her body (weight shifted, soft eyes, loose mouth). By the end of the session she was offering full relaxed behavior within 2-3 minutes. This is a skill that will serve her well in coffee shops, vets, and any public environment.

excerpt: Off-leash heeling session. Lola is starting to understand heel position.
full_text: Introduced formal heel position today — dog's right shoulder aligned with the handler's left leg, both moving together. Lola understood the concept quickly but anticipates the "heel" cue and moves before she's asked. We'll work on building the behavior on cue rather than on expectation. Off-leash heel in a low-distraction environment was solid. Looking forward to building duration.

excerpt: Lola had a quiet day care — seemed a bit low energy, may be under the weather.
full_text: Lola was noticeably quieter than usual today. She engaged in play for short bursts but mostly chose to rest near the staff. No signs of distress, just low energy. She ate her treat at pickup so appetite seems fine. May just be an off day. Worth monitoring at home — if she seems lethargic for more than a day or two, a vet check would be a good idea.

excerpt: Brodie had a great reactivity session. Thresholds are getting wider.
full_text: Worked on bike reactivity today using a systematic desensitization approach. We parked a bicycle across the field and let Brodie acclimate at distance. Once he could look at the bike and look back at me, we moved 5 feet closer. We ended up within 20 feet of a stationary bike with Brodie totally relaxed — huge progress from six weeks ago when he'd lunge at 50 feet. The goal is moving bikes eventually, but stationary is the right first step.

excerpt: Brodie is rock-solid on CGC foundation skills. Working toward test readiness.
full_text: CGC prep session today. Ran through all 10 items on the test: accepting a friendly stranger, sitting politely for petting, appearance and grooming, walking on a loose leash, walking through a crowd, sit and down on command, stay in position, coming when called, reaction to another dog, reaction to distractions, and supervised separation. Brodie passed 8 of 10 with flying colors. The two we're still working on: walking through a crowd (he slows way down and wants to greet everyone) and reaction to distractions (skateboards specifically).

excerpt: Loose-leash walking in a crowd is improving. Brodie walked past 4 strangers without stopping.
full_text: Worked on the "walking through a crowd" component of the CGC test. Set up a mock crowd with 4 staff members and had Brodie walk a path through them. He stopped to solicit petting from two of them, which is a fault on the test. But by the third pass he walked straight through with a slight wag and no stops. That's real progress for a social dog who loves people. The trick is channeling his social energy into walking forward, not stopping.

excerpt: First focused reactivity session with Brodie. Good baseline established.
full_text: Today was an assessment session for Brodie's bike/skateboard reactivity. Threshold is about 40 feet for a stationary bike and probably 60+ feet for a moving one. He doesn't show aggression — it's all noise and forward pressure, classic frustration barking. His body language relaxes quickly once the trigger is gone, which is a good sign. Plan: systematic desensitization at distance, working toward the moving trigger over 6-8 sessions. He's on Carprofen so we'll keep sessions short and avoid lots of repeated positioning.

excerpt: Supervised separation exercise — Brodie was calm for the full 3 minutes.
full_text: CGC supervised separation: handed Brodie's leash to a neutral stranger and walked out of sight for 3 minutes. He looked for me for the first 30 seconds, then settled into a loose sit. No vocalizing, no pawing, no attempts to follow. That's a pass. He's a confident dog who trusts that people come back. Worked on "sit for greeting" afterward — he's learned that sitting = the person comes to him, which is a nice payoff for a social dog.

excerpt: Brodie walked past two dogs on leash without pulling. That's a first.
full_text: Big milestone today: Brodie walked past two on-leash dogs without pulling or stopping to sniff. We used a parallel walking exercise at 10 feet of lateral distance and gradually reduced to 5 feet. He kept checking back with me throughout, which is exactly right. His focus is really strong for a 7-year-old with a lifetime of leash pulling habits. The counter-conditioning is working.

excerpt: Reactivity session 3 — Brodie stayed under threshold with a slow-moving bike at 30 feet.
full_text: Third reactivity session and we hit a real milestone: Brodie stayed under threshold while a bike slowly rolled past at 30 feet. He noticed it, his ears went up, but he turned back to me for a treat instead of reacting. That's the behavior we're looking for — notice the trigger, check in with handler, get rewarded. We'll keep working at this distance until it's totally automatic, then start reducing distance.

excerpt: Grooming and handling acceptance. Brodie was patient through a full mock exam.
full_text: CGC prep for the grooming and handling item. Mock vet exam: approached from the front, checked ears, looked at teeth, touched paws, ran hands along the back. Brodie was relaxed throughout — clearly has been handled this way since puppyhood. He's on the older side so some mild stiffness when repositioning is expected; the Carprofen is keeping him comfortable. No issues with this item.

excerpt: Down-stay at 20 feet for 30 seconds. Brodie is reliable on the fundamentals.
full_text: Down-stay work today: 20 feet of distance, 30 seconds duration, with a stranger walking nearby as a distraction. Brodie passed all three attempts. His downs are fast and confident — no reluctance, no creeping. That's partly his age and partly good training history. The joint support from the Carprofen means he can get up and down comfortably, which is worth noting for anyone worried about asking him to down frequently.

excerpt: Assessment session for Brodie's CGC readiness. He's closer than expected.
full_text: Initial CGC assessment for Brodie. He came in with strong foundation skills — sit, down, stay, come, loose-leash basics. He's an older dog with good habits. The areas to work on are reactivity to moving objects (bikes, skateboards), walking politely through a crowd without stopping, and the supervised separation. Everything else is test-ready or close. Suggested 6-8 sessions focused on the reactivity piece, which is the hardest item for him.
